metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: AC6 specifies an integration test using 'httpx `ASGITransport` wrapping the MCP server', but `build_server()` returns a `FastMCP` (stdio-transport) instance — no ASGI interface is established anywhere in the codebase, and ALL existing integration tests invoke the server by calling `srv.call_tool()` / `srv.list_tools()` directly, never via HTTP/ASGI. If `FastMCP` does not expose an ASGI app, the integration test as written cannot be wired up; the plan doesn't explain how to obtain the ASGI callable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6 (integration test) / src/rebar/mcp_server.py build_server()

findings.finding: The Security section mandates that private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, `169.254.0.0/16`) are 'rejected at construction' but never specifies the detection mechanism — whether the check is lexical (URL string / `urllib.parse` hostname extraction + `ipaddress` literal matching) or DNS-resolution-based (resolve the hostname and check the resulting IP). The two approaches have materially different security properties: a lexical check catches `192.168.1.1` but passes `internal-as.corp` (which resolves to a private IP), so a lexical-only implementation is bypassable by using a private hostname instead of a bare IP. Leaving the mechanism implementation-defined means the security control may not achieve its stated goal.
findings.location: Security section — first bullet: 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts … are rejected by default'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The private-host check parses the literal IP address from the URL (via `ipaddress.ip_address(urllib.parse.urlparse(endpoint).hostname)`); hostnames are NOT DNS-resolved at construction time — an operator using a private hostname must set `allow_private_host=true` (documented risk).' OR: 'The check resolves the hostname at construction (via `socket.getaddrinfo`) and rejects any result that is_private/is_loopback/is_link_local.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'resolved at implementation time' with the concrete FastMCP attribute/method that exposes the Streamable-HTTP ASGI app (e.g., `fastmcp_instance.get_asgi_app()` or whichever call S2's integration tests already use), so the executor has a deterministic starting point.
findings.finding: The integration test AC defers the method for obtaining the ASGI application from FastMCP to the executor with the phrase 'resolved at implementation time', which is a placeholder deferring a real design/API-discovery decision rather than specifying a concrete entrypoint.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — integration test AC (AC6): 'obtained from the configured FastMCP via its Streamable-HTTP entrypoint (resolved at implementation time)'
findings.suggested_fix: Inspect S2's integration test for how it obtains the ASGI app from the FastMCP instance and paste the exact call here, e.g.: 'obtained via `app = server.streamable_http_app()` (the same call S2 uses in its integration test)'.

findings.finding: The plan never specifies the fail-closed behavior when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at runtime. If the verifier proceeds to make an introspection call with no credential (or an empty password), the `client_secret_basic` authentication guarantee on the boundary-crossing path is silently dropped — and if the AS accepts unauthenticated introspection, token validation becomes unauthenticated.
findings.location: Security section — 'client_secret_basic, secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env'; What section — 'auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section add: 'If the env var named by auth_introspection_client_secret_env is unset or empty at construction time, construction raises ConfigError — fail-closed.' Add an AC5 sub-case: 'and that constructing the verifier when the named env var is absent raises ConfigError (observable oracle: ConfigError at construction, never a verifier instance).'

findings.finding: The fallback behavior when `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` names an environment variable that is absent or empty at runtime is entirely unspecified. The plan defines the config key as holding the NAME of an env var (not the secret itself), but provides no decision rule for the secret-resolution failure path: should construction of `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` raise immediately? Should it raise at first call? What exception type? This is a structural gap in the sub-step failure protocol.
findings.location: What section — '`auth_introspection_client_secret_env` (NAME of an env var holding the client secret — no plaintext secret in config)'; Security section — 'secret sourced from `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'If the environment variable named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at construction time, raise `ConfigError` immediately (fail-closed, fail-fast — never send an empty credential).' Add a corresponding AC5 sub-bullet: 'construction raises when the named env var is missing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the private-host guard applies only at construction time (current spec) or also at call time against the resolved IP — if construction-only, document the DNS-rebinding limitation in the Security section so the implementer and future reviewers understand the residual risk.
findings.finding: The private-host guard is checked at construction time against the URL hostname string, but not at call time after DNS resolution. A hostname that passes the construction check (e.g. `as.internal.example.com`) can resolve to an RFC 1918 address at runtime — a DNS rebinding scenario that bypasses the guard entirely. The plan is silent on whether the call-time resolved IP should also be checked.
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, 169.254.0.0/16) are rejected by default'; AC5 — 'a private/link-local host is rejected by default'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Security section: 'The private-host guard resolves only the literal hostname in the configured URL at construction time; it does not re-check the DNS-resolved IP at call time (a future hardening could use httpx event hooks to inspect the connected address). Operators in hostile-DNS environments should pin the AS IP or use a private CA with hostname pinning.'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the behavior when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at construction time, and no AC tests this guard — yet the security requirement ('no plaintext secret in config') implies a hard failure is required.
findings.location: What section ('auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret — no plaintext secret in config)'); AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'and that constructing the verifier when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is unset raises `ConfigurationError` at construction time (observable oracle: `pytest.raises(ConfigurationError)`).'; add one sentence to the Security section: 'If the env var is absent or empty at construction time, raise `ConfigurationError` immediately — never defer to call time.'

findings.finding: The construction-time private-host rejection mechanism only works for literal IP addresses in the endpoint URL; a hostname (e.g. `internal-auth.corp.local`) that resolves to an RFC 1918 or loopback address at DNS lookup time will pass the construction guard silently, making the SSRF protection ineffective for the most common real-world internal AS deployment pattern.
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts … are rejected by default'; AC5 — 'a private/link-local host is rejected … (observable oracle: rejection at construction for the http/private cases)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Security section: 'The construction-time guard checks the literal host component against private/loopback/link-local IP ranges using `ipaddress`; a hostname that resolves to a private IP is **not** caught at construction (documented known gap). Operators relying on `allow_private_host=false` should configure the endpoint as a literal-IP URL or enforce network-level controls.' Update AC5 to explicitly scope the oracle to literal-IP rejection so the implementation is not silently incomplete.

findings.finding: The private-host SSRF guard is specified as 'rejected at construction' and the AC5 oracle is 'rejection at construction for the http/private cases,' but Python's ipaddress module (the natural implementation tool) can only classify literal IP strings — a hostname like `internal-as.corp.local` that resolves to 10.x.x.x at connect time bypasses the construction-time guard silently, violating the stated security invariant that 'private/link-local endpoint hosts are rejected by default.'
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, 169.254.0.0/16) are rejected by default'; AC5 — 'a private/link-local host is rejected by default … observable oracle: rejection at construction for the http/private cases'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Security section: 'The construction-time private-host check validates literal IP addresses in the URL; for hostname endpoints, a custom httpx transport (or event hook) that resolves the target IP and checks it against the RFC-1918/loopback/link-local ranges before sending is required to close the SSRF path for the hostname case.' Extend AC5: 'and that a hostname resolving to a private IP is also rejected (or document the limitation explicitly and note reliance on network-layer controls).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly that if the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent from the environment, the verifier raises at construction time (fail-fast), and add a corresponding unit test assertion.
findings.finding: The plan does not state what happens at construction time when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is not set in the environment — it is ambiguous whether this is a construction-time error or a runtime error at first introspection call.
findings.location: What section / Security section: 'secret sourced from `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'If the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is not present in `os.environ` at construction, the verifier raises `ValueError` immediately (fail-fast).' Add an AC5 sub-bullet: 'construction raises if the named env var is absent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: introspection transport errors (non-200, timeout, malformed response) must emit a structured log entry at ERROR level (without token/secret values) distinguishing the failure type, and an AC line must assert this logging occurs.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (logging, metrics, or structured error signals) for introspection endpoint failures. Operators cannot distinguish between 'token is invalid' and 'Authorization Server is unavailable', making production diagnosis of auth outages invisible.
findings.location: Security section / Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `httpx` needs to be added to `[project.optional-dependencies].mcp` in `pyproject.toml`, and specify that the `import httpx` in `_mcp_auth.py` must be lazy (inside a method/function body, not at module scope) to satisfy the enforced `test_no_core_module_imports_heavy_stack_at_module_scope` optionality gate.
findings.finding: The plan states 'Reuse the already-present httpx' as if httpx is a declared dependency of the mcp extra, but httpx is not in [project.dependencies] or [project.optional-dependencies].mcp. It only arrives transitively via pydantic-ai-slim in [agents]. More critically, test_core_optionality.py explicitly lists 'httpx' in _HEAVY and enforces that it must not be imported at module scope anywhere under src/rebar/ — a rule the new _mcp_auth.py will be subject to. The plan does not address whether httpx needs to be added to the mcp extra declaration in pyproject.toml, nor does it specify that the httpx import in _mcp_auth.py must be lazy (inside __init__ or the verify method), leaving both the dependency contract and the enforced optionality test at risk.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale section ('Reuse the already-present httpx'); pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies].mcp
findings.suggested_fix: Add `httpx` to the `mcp` extra in pyproject.toml (e.g., `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx>=0.24"]`) and ensure all `import httpx` statements inside `_mcp_auth.py` are inside function/method bodies, not at module scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that S2 (the dependency that introduces `auth_strategies` and `auth_resource_server_url`) is either already merged or concurrently scoped; if neither, add explicit blocking dependency language and confirm the `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']` additions from S2 are part of the combined deliverable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `auth_strategies` (the strategy-selection config key) and `auth_resource_server_url` are 'introduced by S2, this story's dependency'. Zero evidence of either exists anywhere in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, `McpConfig`, `_SECTIONS['mcp']`, or `mcp_server.py`. No S2 ticket resolves in the tracker. The introspection verifier's activation path (`introspection` in `mcp.auth_strategies`) and its audience-validation target (`auth_resource_server_url`) are both entirely absent from the current codebase, and S2 itself cannot be confirmed as a delivered or even existing story.
findings.location: What / Scope sections — 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**, this story's dependency)' and 'stamps `resource=auth_resource_server_url` so the composite check passes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan section that enumerates the exact `McpConfig` fields and `_SECTIONS['mcp']` coercer entries S2 must contribute before S4 can integrate; or, if S2 is effectively merged into S4's scope, add them here explicitly rather than cross-referencing a story that cannot be confirmed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether S1's 'fix the generators to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`' for `gen_env_registry.py` is delivered before S4 lands; if not, specify that S4's implementation must read each `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_*` var via a literal `os.environ` call (not only through the config layer) so the existing AST scanner catches it, or add the `gen_env_registry.py` change to S4's own scope.
findings.finding: The plan states '`env-vars.md` are regenerated for the full key set by S7; S1 fixes the generators to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`'. The actual `gen_env_registry.py` currently generates `docs/env-vars.md` by AST-scanning literal `os.environ` reads in source files — it does NOT iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`. If the new `auth_introspection_*` env vars are read through the config layer (not as bare `os.environ.get('REBAR_MCP_AUTH_INTROSPECTION_...')` literals in `_mcp_auth.py`), the scanner will miss them and the env-vars.md drift gate will fail or silently omit them. S1's fix to 'iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`' is unverified as delivered.
findings.location: What section — '`scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes. (The generated `mcp-reference.md`/`env-vars.md` are regenerated for the full key set by S7; S1 fixes the generators to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`.')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the dynamically-read client-secret env var (the value of `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`) will appear in `docs/env-vars.md`: either add a helper registered in `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`, document a manual annotation, or explicitly acknowledge that only the config key (not the pointed-to env var) is registered.
findings.finding: The `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` config key stores the **name** of an environment variable (not its value), so the actual secret is read via a dynamic key (`os.environ.get(config.auth_introspection_client_secret_env)`). The `gen_env_registry.py` scanner only picks up string-literal env reads; this dynamic read is invisible to it. The plan says `env-vars.md` is regenerated by S7 but neither S4 nor S7 enumerates adding the pointed-to secret env var to `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` or otherwise ensuring the drift gate remains green.
findings.location: What section — `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`; also `env-vars.md` mention

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the private-host guard resolves the hostname to an IP at each request (or pins at construction via a custom httpx transport) rather than only checking the URL string at construction time, to prevent DNS-rebinding SSRF bypass on the outbound introspection call.
findings.finding: The private-host/RFC 1918/link-local check is specified as happening 'at construction' (a static URL string check). This does not defend against DNS rebinding: a hostname that resolves to a public IP at construction time can later resolve to a private/internal IP (due to TTL expiry, DNS cache poisoning, or a misconfigured zone), routing subsequent introspection POSTs to internal services. The plan does not specify resolving and checking the target IP at each request or configuring the httpx transport to prevent SSRF at the network layer.
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, 169.254.0.0/16) are rejected by default; an operator with a legitimate internal AS sets auth_introspection_allow_private_host=true to opt out.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a requirement that the implementation either (a) resolves the configured hostname to an IP at construction and pins the IP in the httpx transport (so DNS changes don't reroute calls), or (b) installs a custom httpx transport that checks `socket.getaddrinfo()` results against the RFC 1918 / loopback / link-local blocks before each connection and raises if a private IP is returned — making the guard runtime-enforced rather than construction-time-only.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reuse the already-present httpx' to justify using httpx directly in `_mcp_auth.py`, but httpx is NOT a declared dependency of the `[mcp]` extra (`pyproject.toml` line 82: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]`). The codebase's optionality CI (`test_core_optionality.py`) explicitly classifies `httpx` as a heavy dep that arrives only via `[agents]`/pydantic-ai and must stay lazy everywhere. A user who installs `nava-rebar[mcp]` without `[agents]` will get an `ImportError` at runtime when the introspection verifier imports httpx.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'; pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies] mcp
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"httpx>=0.27"` to the `mcp` extra in pyproject.toml: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx>=0.27"]`. Alternatively, if mcp>=1.9 already requires httpx as a transitive dep, add a comment in pyproject.toml and the plan naming that transitive chain explicitly so it cannot silently break on an mcp version bump.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify `Optional[str] = None` (or equivalent) defaults for all three new string fields (`auth_introspection_endpoint`, `auth_introspection_client_id`, `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`) in the McpConfig dataclass description, and confirm no rollback is needed because the change is truly backward-compatible with those defaults.
findings.finding: Adding new fields to the McpConfig dataclass without explicitly specifying their defaults risks breaking existing callers that instantiate McpConfig positionally. The plan states the booleans default to false but is silent on the defaults for the three string fields (auth_introspection_endpoint, auth_introspection_client_id, auth_introspection_client_secret_env). If any of these fields lack defaults, every existing McpConfig construction site breaks — a non-additive schema change that contradicts the plan's own 'Additive' claim.
findings.location: What section / 'Adds the auth_introspection_* fields to the McpConfig dataclass' / Compatibility section
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, append: 'All new McpConfig string fields default to None (Optional[str]); the booleans default to False. No existing McpConfig construction site is affected because no positional layout changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a scope item (or AC line) requiring a short ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing: (1) why a bespoke verifier is written rather than importing the SDK class, (2) the no-caching-v1 rationale and the revocation-delay tradeoff, and (3) the audience-validation fail-closed default.
findings.finding: The decision to write a bespoke IntrospectionTokenVerifier rather than use or extend the SDK's class is an architectural divergence that is explained inline in the plan but is not captured in any persistent design artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design doc). Similarly, the 'no caching in v1' policy and the chosen audience-validation semantics are novel cross-component business rules with security implications whose rationale will be invisible to future maintainers who do not read this story's ticket.
findings.location: What section — NOTE paragraph; Security section — 'No caching in v1' and Audience sub-section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add optional config keys `auth_introspection_connect_timeout_s` and `auth_introspection_read_timeout_s` (integers/floats, defaults 5 and 10 respectively) to the McpConfig field list and _SECTIONS['mcp'] coercer, so timeout tuning does not require a code change.
findings.finding: The HTTP timeout values (connect 5 s, read 10 s) are asserted as hardcoded defaults with no config key to override them. These are evolvable operational thresholds — internal AS deployments with different latency profiles, or environments behind proxies, will need tuning — but the plan provides no mechanism to change them without a code change.
findings.location: Security section — 'a bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section config-key list: `auth_introspection_connect_timeout_s` (float, default 5.0) and `auth_introspection_read_timeout_s` (float, default 10.0).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the httpx client is created once at `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` construction (enabling connection pooling/reuse across requests) and that a `close()`/`aclose()` cleanup path is defined (e.g., called on server shutdown or via context manager), documented in the plan's Approach or a new Lifecycle sub-section.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the lifecycle of the httpx client used by the verifier — whether it is created once at construction (enabling connection reuse/pooling) or per-call, and whether it has a defined shutdown/close path. A per-request client is wasteful and forgoes connection pooling; a shared client without a close path leaks connection-pool resources.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale / Security section / AC (integration test AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'The verifier creates one `httpx.AsyncClient` (or `httpx.Client` if the integration is sync) at construction time, stored as `self._client`. The server's shutdown hook calls `await self._client.aclose()`. The integration test's stateful fake is injected by passing a pre-configured client to the constructor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `build_server()` (or the S2 auth-configuration helper it delegates to) as an explicit edit target and describe how the `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` is wired into the FastMCP instance.
findings.finding: `build_server()` in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` is an unenumerated consumer: the plan creates an `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` and wires it via the S2 strategy-selection seam, but the plan never specifies that `build_server()` (or some auth-configuration helper it calls) must be updated to pass the verifier into the FastMCP instance. Without this, the verifier is never activated regardless of config.
findings.location: What / Scope / AC — no mention of `build_server()` as an edit target

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rule to the Security / Expiry section for the `exp=0` edge case: specify whether a present-but-zero `exp` is treated as absent (coerced to `None`) or rejected (token treated as non-active), to ensure the 'Never emit expires_at=0' invariant is implemented unambiguously.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Never emit `expires_at=0`' and '`exp` absent → `None`', but does not specify what to do when the AS returns a present `exp` field with value `0` (a valid NumericDate, though nonsensical for an active token). The implementation could reasonably either pass `0` through (violating 'never emit expires_at=0') or silently coerce it to `None` (treating it as absent), and both behaviors have downstream consequences for how the SDK interprets token validity.
findings.location: Security section — 'Never emit expires_at=0 (the SDK reads a falsy value as no-expiry while looking like a set value)'; 'exp→expires_at (absent → None, per Expiry below)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Expiry bullet: 'If exp is present but equals 0 (pathological AS response), treat it as absent — set expires_at=None rather than passing the zero through.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the SDK (`mcp>=1.9`) does NOT export a production-hardened `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` in any public submodule (e.g., `mcp.server.auth.*`), and document the outcome — if one exists, justify the custom implementation explicitly against it; if absent, confirm this in the plan as a positive assertion.
findings.finding: The plan's core 'write our own verifier' rationale rests on the assertion that the SDK's IntrospectionTokenVerifier is 'an example, not an exported/hardened class' — but this is unverified. The installed SDK exposes mcp.server.auth.provider (with TokenVerifier and AccessToken), and a production IntrospectionTokenVerifier may exist in the SDK's auth submodules. If the SDK already exports a hardened class, the plan reinvents it unnecessarily; if it truly is example-only, the plan's choice is correct — but the plan does not cite a resolution to this question.
findings.location: What section — 'NOTE: the SDK's IntrospectionTokenVerifier is an example, not an exported/hardened class — this story WRITES its own'
findings.suggested_fix: Run `python -c "import mcp; import pkgutil; print([m.name for m in pkgutil.walk_packages(mcp.__path__, prefix='mcp.') if 'introspect' in m.name.lower()])"` against the installed mcp>=1.9 package to enumerate introspection-related submodules, and add a one-line note to the plan's Approach section confirming the outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `client_secret_basic` is supported by all named target AS providers (Keycloak, Okta, Auth0, WorkOS) for their `/introspect` endpoint, OR acknowledge in the plan that `client_secret_post` is out of scope for v1 and add it to the documented follow-on optimizations list.
findings.finding: The plan mandates `client_secret_basic` as the sole client-authentication method for the introspection endpoint, without acknowledging that RFC 7662 §2.1 and many real-world AS deployments (e.g., Auth0, Okta) also support or even require `client_secret_post`. The plan names Keycloak, Okta, and Auth0 as target providers but does not verify whether `client_secret_basic` is universally available across all of them. Choosing exclusively `client_secret_basic` without a documented prior-art check against the named providers' requirements risks interoperability gaps at deployment.
findings.location: Security section — 'client_secret_basic, secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env'; Why section — 'Keycloak, Okta, Auth0, WorkOS'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and name the exact FastMCP method/attribute that exposes the Streamable-HTTP ASGI app (e.g. by inspecting the installed FastMCP source or its changelog), then replace 'resolved at implementation time' in AC6 with the pinned API call.
findings.finding: The AC6 integration test requires the Streamable-HTTP ASGI app from a FastMCP instance, but the plan names no concrete FastMCP method or attribute that exposes it, deferring the resolution to 'implementation time'. Probing `mcp.server.fastmcp` for likely candidates (`streamable_http_app`, `get_asgi_app`, `http_app`, `streamable_http`) returns UNRESOLVED for all of them. This is a new integration surface (Streamable-HTTP transport of FastMCP, distinct from the already-used stdio transport) whose specific API is unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 (integration test): 'the ASGI app, obtained from the configured FastMCP via its Streamable-HTTP entrypoint (resolved at implementation time), NOT `build_server()`'s return directly'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm S2 is merged and `mcp.auth_strategies` is present in `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']` before beginning S4 implementation, or explicitly add delivering that config key to S4's scope with a note that it is standing in for the unmerged S2 seam.
findings.finding: The plan's activation mechanism depends on `mcp.auth_strategies`, described as 'the strategy-selection config key introduced by S2, this story's dependency'. This key is confirmed absent from the codebase: `McpConfig` has only `readonly`, `allow_llm`, and `allow_jira_sync`; `_SECTIONS['mcp']` has exactly those three coercers; no `auth_strategies` appears anywhere under `src/rebar`. The integration as described — 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list' — is not achievable until S2 is merged.
findings.location: What section: 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**, this story's dependency — not a pre-existing seam)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard prerequisite gate to the plan: 'S2 must be merged and `mcp.auth_strategies` must be present in `McpConfig` before this story starts'. Alternatively, if S4 is intended to land before S2, scope S4 to also add the `auth_strategies` field and coercer, and update the dependency note accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require that exceptions raised on non-200/timeout/malformed introspection responses are constructed without including the raw response body or the submitted token value in their message/args, so the 'Tokens/secrets never logged' guarantee holds through exception propagation paths as well as direct logger calls.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a non-200, timeout, or malformed response 'raises' but does not require that raised exception messages exclude the raw HTTP response body or the submitted token value. Some AS implementations echo the submitted token in error responses; if the exception carries the full response body, the token value can leak to the composite verifier's caller (exception propagation, error telemetry, or an unguarded logging call higher in the stack). The plan's 'Tokens/secrets never logged' statement covers logging but not exception object content.
findings.location: Security section — 'Fail-closed transport: a non-200, timeout, or malformed response raises'; 'Tokens/secrets never logged'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'Exceptions raised on transport errors MUST NOT include the raw response body or the token value in their message — use a fixed error class (e.g. IntrospectionError("introspection endpoint returned non-200")) with no body interpolation, and sanitise the token from any exception context before raising.'

findings.finding: The Security section requires a 'bounded timeout' on the introspection HTTP call but states no concrete value or range; AC4 tests that a timeout causes a raise but never specifies the timeout duration to configure or assert, making the 'bounded' property unmeasurable and leaving an executor free to pick an arbitrary value.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded timeout'); AC4 ('non-200 / timeout / malformed response raises')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section replace 'bounded timeout' with 'a 10-second total timeout (connect + read)'; add a constructor parameter `timeout_seconds: float = 10.0` to `McpConfig` or the verifier constructor, and extend AC5 with: 'and that the httpx client is constructed with `timeout=10.0` (observable oracle: `client.timeout.read == 10.0`)'.

findings.finding: The plan says 'bounded timeout' but never specifies the concrete timeout value, unit, or an AC done-definition. An implementer could set 30 s and still satisfy the spec, blocking the hot path for every token validation.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; AC has no corresponding timeout oracle
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security: 'Default timeout: 5 s (configurable via auth_introspection_timeout_ms); raises TimeoutError after that bound.' Add an AC: 'Unit test asserts the verifier raises within the configured timeout when the fake AS stalls.'

findings.finding: The plan states 'bounded timeout' for the introspection HTTP call but never specifies the timeout value, its default, or whether it is a configurable key — so operators cannot tune it and no AC verifies it.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded timeout'); Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers timeout value or config)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_introspection_timeout` (int, default 5) to the config key list alongside the other `auth_introspection_*` keys, and add an AC line: 'Unit test asserts the httpx introspection request is constructed with a timeout equal to `auth_introspection_timeout`; a fake that never responds triggers the timeout and raises within that bound.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'bounded timeout' for the introspection HTTP call but declares no timeout value, no configurable field, and no default. An unspecified or excessively long timeout allows a slow or adversarial Authorization Server to hold open httpx connections indefinitely, creating a resource-exhaustion vector on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; Approach & rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard-coded default (e.g., `timeout=5.0`) to the httpx POST call, document it in the Security section, and extend AC3's 'non-200 / timeout / malformed response raises' oracle to include a slow-response scenario. Optionally expose it as `auth_introspection_timeout` (seconds) in the config list.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
